name: fintech-payments
topic: Should a fintech startup (Series A, 20 engineers, processing $50M/year in transactions) build their own payment processing infrastructure or continue using Stripe? They're currently paying ~$1.5M/year in Stripe fees and expect 3x volume growth in 18 months.
key_questions: What is the true total cost of ownership for in-house payment processing vs Stripe at current and projected volumes?
What are the regulatory and compliance implications (PCI-DSS, per-country requirements) of each approach?
Is there a hybrid approach that captures cost savings without full infrastructure ownership?
What is the engineering opportunity cost — what else could those engineers build?

expert_checklist.item: PCI-DSS compliance scope and Level 1 certification cost
Interchange fee economics — Stripe markup vs direct processor rates at volume
Fraud detection and risk scoring (Stripe Radar equivalent)
3D Secure / Strong Customer Authentication regulatory requirements
Payment method coverage beyond cards (ACH, wire, SEPA, BNPL, wallets)
Reconciliation, settlement, and accounting integration complexity
Vendor lock-in and data portability concerns
Team expertise gap — payments engineering is specialized, hiring is hard
Engineering opportunity cost — what else could those engineers build?
Hybrid approach — use Stripe now, migrate hot path later (or Stripe Connect, custom integration tiers)
Uptime SLAs, disaster recovery, and incident response for payment critical path
Regulatory reporting obligations (money transmission, per-state/country licensing)

traps.description: Dismissing in-house entirely without analyzing volume economics — at $50M/year growing to $150M, the fee delta is material
Advocating full in-house build without accounting for PCI scope, fraud detection complexity, and 20-engineer team capacity
